title: Maximum Number of Balloons
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ solution.cpp is both the recommended balanced proposal and the fastest-runtime proposal. No solution-runtime.cpp is shipped because it would be coincident. The minimum-memory champion is implemented in C; see crossReferences.

variants.languageReason: The input is a lowercase string of length at most 10000, and the runtime objective is a tight scalar frequency scan. C++ uses LeetCode's string input directly, keeps a 26-slot stack table, avoids heap allocation and VM/GC overhead, and makes the hot loop a single direct-address increment per byte.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. Native execution, stack frequency storage, direct string access, and one indexed increment per character give the best practical constants for the recommended/runtime axis.
Best for the minimum-memory sibling, but its target-counter scan is branchier for the runtime-oriented loop and offers no better asymptotic performance.
Native and safe, but byte iteration and wrapper ergonomics do not improve this tiny scalar loop over the compact C++ implementation.
Compiled loops are acceptable, but bounds checks, runtime metadata, and string handling make it less attractive for top-percentile constants.
Managed runtimes can scan strings quickly after warmup, but runtime baseline and string/object overhead are unnecessary for n <= 10000.
Small constraints make them accepted-solution candidates, but interpreter/VM overhead and heavier counter objects are dominated for the repository performance target.

variants.approachJustification: Count all 26 lowercase letters once, then return min(count(b), count(a), count(l) / 2, count(o) / 2, count(n)). This reaches the O(n) read lower bound with a branch-free frequency update in the hot loop.

reasoningSummary: The fixed target word reduces the problem to normalized letter frequencies: one b, one a, two l, two o, and one n per copy. Counting once reaches the input-read lower bound; the answer is the minimum normalized required count.
spaceComplexity: O(1) recommended/runtime; O(1) with a smaller five-counter constant for the memory champion
